name: pre-dispatch agent config — orchestrator must run bar build agent before fanning out subagents
task_prompt:
Run `bar sequence show frame-debug` and execute the sequence on the following problem. Follow the sequence protocol exactly — run bar build for each step as required.

Problem: A distributed payment service is intermittently dropping transactions during peak load. The drop rate correlates with database connection pool exhaustion in the order service, but the checkout service logs show timeouts before the pool limit is reached. The queue consumer in the notification service appears to lag during these windows. Reproducing the issue requires load > 500 req/s.
eval_gate: Before the dispatch step fans out to subagents, the transcript must contain a bar build tool call result that includes the 'agent' channel token (the result contains '=== TASK 任務 (DO THIS) ===') appearing after the frame enumeration output. Additionally, the Agent tool call(s) that spawn the per-frame subagents must contain a verbatim excerpt from that bar build agent output — confirming the output was actually used as the subagent prompt, not merely produced and discarded. Absence of the bar build agent result, or its absence from the spawning Agent tool call text, is a Criterion G failure.
notes: Tests Criterion G: pre-dispatch agent config. The orchestrator must synthesize a task-specific bar build agent command, produce a system prompt, and inject a verbatim excerpt of that output into each spawned Agent tool call. Without the pre-dispatch agent config protocol in bar-workflow skill guidance, agents skip directly from frame enumeration to subagent launch with no custom system prompt. The fix adds guidance to bar-workflow: 'Before executing any dispatch step, run bar build [task-appropriate tokens] agent and include a verbatim excerpt of the output in the prompt of each spawned subagent.' Two-part failure mode: (1) bar build agent not run at all; (2) bar build agent run but output not included in Agent tool call — both are Criterion G failures.
